meta.title: Inalpha — Quant agents that evolve under audit.
meta.description: Inalpha is an open-source quant agent framework — an oracle that keeps a ledger. Agents pick the factors that work now (ranked by time-series Rank IC), convene a panel of investing legends for opposing research, write and evolve strategy code, and route every order through machine approval — the LLM has no direct path to the order book. One strategy codebase runs across backtest, paper, and the live runner, with multi-market routing and a Claude Code-style hooks / permissions / plan-exec layer that make every decision provable and replayable.

hero.title: Quant agents
hero.tagline: An oracle that keeps a ledger.
hero.sub: Agents pick the factors that work now, convene a panel of investing legends, write and evolve the strategies — and route every order through machine approval. The LLM writes the code; the engineering harness signs every decision.

problem.title: LLM-written strategies are easy.
problem.sub: Most "LLM + quant" stacks today: prompt in, "long BTC, conf 0.62" out, broker SDK next door. Lost money — no way to find which prompt or which slice of data broke. Made money — no way to tell alpha from overfit. Inalpha brings the Claude Code engineering harness (hooks / permissions / plan-exec) to LLM quant — the LLM only writes code; every decision is signed, every backtest is replayable, and the order path is never reachable by the LLM directly.

lineage.blackbox: "long BTC, conf 0.62" — source data, prompt, model version: nowhere on record. Nothing to attribute when it loses
lineage.inalpha: research_id → backtest_run_id → plan_id, every decision traceable back through its full UUID chain

replay.blackbox: How was last week's sharpe 2.14 computed? Params forgotten, data slice forgotten — recompute from memory
replay.inalpha: strategy_id + params_hash + as_of as a fingerprint. Same numbers any day, any week

guardrail.blackbox: The LLM holds the broker SDK directly. One jailbreak prompt, one hallucination = real money out
guardrail.inalpha: permissions.yaml deny + plan/exec one-shot TTL token. The order path is unreachable by the LLM, ever

thesis.title: Two non-negotiables.

items: Factor / risk / evolution agents, each with its own toolset
Opposing stances in research debate by design
columnA.footer: Not just an LLM call wrapped in a UI.

columnB.header: B. Engineering discipline is the floor
items: Plan → approve → execute with one-shot token
Decision records on disk, declarative configs in repo

thesis.conclusion: A alone is just a chat wrapper. B alone is just another backtest framework. Both — that's Inalpha.

wedge.title: Most AI trading is a black box.

lose.blackbox: "Buy BTC, 0.62 confidence" — and no way to tell which data or which step went wrong.
lose.inalpha: Replay the exact decision: the data it saw, the logic it ran, the reason it gave.

win.blackbox: Was that skill, or luck? If you can't reproduce it, you can't trust it.
win.inalpha: Every idea and test is on record, so real edge is something you can prove — not a lucky streak.

trigger.blackbox: The model holds the keys to your account. One bad prompt becomes a real order.
trigger.inalpha: The AI only proposes. A rule you set places the order — it can't pull the trigger itself.

wedge.close: Every step explained. Every trade, your call.

intelligence.title: Agents that pick the factors that work now.
intelligence.sub: Not a hard-coded indicator set. Agents rank factors by time-series Rank IC, surface the ones working right now, and convene opposing analysts before a single line of strategy is written.

timing.heading: Factor timing
timing.body: factor.timing ranks a 79-factor zoo — price, volume, fundamental, and FRED macro families — by rolling Rank IC, and surfaces the signals working right now. Every factor is formalized, IC-tested, multiple-testing-checked, de-correlated, and logged with author, timestamp, and the economic-story gate decision.
timing.caption: factor.timing · rolling Rank IC

panel.heading: A panel of investing legends
panel.body: A deep dive convenes technical, fundamental, and sentiment analysts — plus an optional panel of investing legends — for opposing views that feed a synthesis, never a single oracle voice.

lib.heading: A governed factor library
facts: 79 factors · 7 families · incl. FRED macro (yields · DXY · VIX · CPI)
decay watch · rank_ic_recent vs rank_ic, surfaced per factor
top-N de-correlated at ρ < 0.85 — never ten copies of one signal
cross-section · factor.panel_score ranks a basket by cross-sectional Rank IC — which name, not just when
discovery L1 · whitelisted DSL (no eval/exec) → auto-eval + dup-check + register
lib.panelFoot: 79 factors · de-correlated ρ<0.85 · decay-watched · recent IC vs base

research.title: Research is a debate, not a single voice.
research.body: A deep dive convenes technical, fundamental, sentiment, and valuation analysts — and, when you ask, a panel of investing legends. They argue opposing cases, then Inalpha synthesizes the disagreement into one decision on record.

debate.speaker: technical
debate.text: NVDA broke to new highs — volume is confirming the move.

debate.speaker: sentiment
debate.text: Breakouts fail when the trade is this crowded — everyone is already long.

debate.speaker: Wood
debate.text: Crowded isn't wrong. AI compute demand compounds — the TAM is still underpriced.

debate.speaker: Buffett
debate.text: However big the TAM, price is what you pay. At this multiple, where's the value?

debate.speaker: Lynch
debate.text: Value follows growth here. I know what they sell — the order book holds up.

debate.speaker: Burry
debate.text: The order book? Read the inventory line and the China-revenue footnotes first.

debate.speaker: Druckenmiller
debate.text: Footnotes don't stop a capex cycle — liquidity is with the bulls.

debate.speaker: Marks
debate.text: Both sides are half right. It's not whether NVDA is great — it's whether great is priced in.

debate.speaker: risk
debate.text: Debate noted. Size it for the drawdown you can't time.

research.verdictText: long NVDA · conviction 0.41 · every stance logged
research.legendsLabel: Optional · a panel of investing legends

skills.label: Pluggable methods · agent skills
skills.body: Beyond the panel, agents load research playbooks as skills — a supply-chain lens, a methodology you bring in — read only when a case calls for it. Knowledge plugs in; tokens aren't spent on what isn't in play.

evolution.title: Agents write the strategy — then rewrite it to beat itself.
evolution.body: An agent writes a full strategy in Python. Three sandbox gates clear it before a single backtest runs. Then it mutates under a multi-objective fitness, so no one metric can be gamed — only strategies that beat the baseline survive.

gates.subprocessNote: runs walled off from the kernel

evolution.loop: mutate · evaluate · keep what beats the baseline
evolution.gate: Before promotion a gate scores the candidate against the baseline; below bar, the agent auto-pivots and rewrites once — only what clears ships. Survivors can be cross-validated — WalkForward / Purged / Combinatorial CV with a Deflated Sharpe — so an edge has to hold across many out-of-sample paths, not one lucky window.

robustness.title: Survive the gauntlet,
robustness.sub: Picking the best of many trials is itself how you overfit. So every stage — factor selection, strategy selection, parameters, out-of-sample — carries its own statistical correction, not a single backtest you fell in love with.

layers.kills: a lucky window can't pose as an edge
a data-mined ghost can't reach the library
no single metric can be gamed

items: test fold ends on the latest bar

demo.title: Watch them disagree in real time.
demo.blurb: Prompt in. Four agents with opposing stances, distinct toolsets, separate data. Output is a structured decision record — not a paragraph.

btc.label: BTC / FOMC
btc.prompt: Should we long BTC into the FOMC print?

tsla.label: TSLA / Earnings
tsla.prompt: Short TSLA into earnings — fade the rally?

usdjpy.label: USDJPY
usdjpy.prompt: Is USDJPY 158 a BOJ intervention zone?

spyskew.label: SPY skew
spyskew.prompt: SPY put-call skew steep — buy puts?

kernel.title: One codebase.
kernel.sub: Strategy code is written once. Backtest, paper, and the live runner all share it — swap only the Clock and the Gateway. Business logic never changes; divergence can only come from physical reality — slippage, latency, data precision.

data.title: data
data.desc: Multi-venue feeds. Freshness-anchored. Same client across markets.

paper.title: paper
paper.desc: In-memory matching, backtest engine, persistent paper trading. State is replay-able.

research.title: research
research.desc: Multi-analyst LLM debate. Opposing stances. No stale numbers passed as insight.

trust.title: The AI proposes.
trust.body: Every order intent walks a one-way path: the agent drafts a plan, a rule you set (or you) approves it, and only then does a single-use, expiring token unlock execution. The model has no tool that reaches the order book directly — not after a jailbreak, not after a hallucination.
trust.wall: denied — no direct path for the LLM

guard.label: Framework backstop
guard.body: Independent of strategy logic, the framework caps every position at a −20% hard stop — applied in backtest too, so the drawdown you see is the drawdown you'd live. Its liquidations clear the risk gate only when triple-signed (SELL · guard tag · guard- order prefix), so nothing can spoof its way past the boundary.

harness.title: Inspired by Claude Code.
harness.sub: Declarative configs decide what each agent can call. Plan → approve → execute is enforced by a one-shot, TTL-bound token — the LLM never reaches the order path unsupervised. Subagents isolate risk. MCP plugs in tools without hand-rolled glue.
harness.hint: ── Click a mechanism on the right to inspect real source

chipDescs.permissions: Tool access scoped per agent role.
chipDescs.hooks: Middleware runs on every tool call.
chipDescs.plan-exec: Plan first, execute once, no runaway loops.
chipDescs.subagent: Risk and review live in isolated subagents.
chipDescs.mcp: Model Context Protocol native, no glue code.

samples.permissions:
defaultMode: ask           # fail-closed

allow:
  - "data.*"               # quotes / news / macro (read)
  - "paper.run_backtest"
  - "research.*"
  - "trade.create_plan"    # proposing is fine
  - "trade.approve_plan"
  - "trade.execute_plan"

deny:                        # the LLM cannot bypass these
  - "paper.submit_order"   # legacy direct-order path
  - "live.submit_order(notional>=10000)"
  - "live.emergency_stop_all"
samples.plan-exec:
$ trade.create_plan
    intent=open_long   symbol=BTCUSDT   side=buy   qty=0.05
    rationale="4h breakout · bull thesis holds"
    researchId=rs_8f3a   backtestRunId=bt_91c2     # ← lineage
→ planId=pln_7c91   status=pending   expireAt=+300s

$ trade.approve_plan { planId, reason="risk gate pass" }
→ approvalToken=tk_…   one-shot · 60s TTL

$ trade.execute_plan { planId, approvalToken }
→ ok · orderId=ord_4d2a   token consumed
samples.swarm:
$ swarm.run_backtest_grid
    candidateIds: [c_3a1, c_7c9, c_5e2]   # D-9 LLM-authored
    symbols:      [BTCUSDT, ETHUSDT, SOLUSDT]
    venue: binance   timeframe: 1h
    from: 2025-01-01    to: 2026-05-26

→ grid:     3 × 3 = 9 runs    (cap ≤ 20 by grid-size-cap hook)
→ workers:  6   wall: 4.2s    ok: 9 / errored: 0
→ pareto:   3 points (Sharpe ↑  · maxDD ↓)
→ top1:     c_3a1 on ETHUSDT   fitness=1.84
            baseline(buy_and_hold)=0.92  ·  alpha ✓

coverage.title: Same kernel. Same prompts. Same agents.
coverage.sub: All markets route through one orchestrator. Add a venue, every agent gets it for free.

currentState.title: Where we are honest with you
items: Autonomous paper runner + cross-currency cash: promoted strategies trade a simulated multi-currency account on live bars, machine-approved through plan/exec, with full decision replay
A 79-factor library with lineage tracking and decay alerts — factors are IC-monitored and de-correlated; alerts only warn, they never move your book
Framework position guard: a −20% hard stop applied in backtest and paper alike, independent of strategy logic
RiskEngine enforced at the paper HTTP boundary: notional cap / price deviation / drawdown veto / real exchange calendars across every market
Point-in-time fundamentals: financials filtered by report period + release lag, so a backtest never sees a number before it was public (akshare live; yfinance v1 not yet PIT, flagged in place)
No real-capital deployment — the runner trades a paper account, orders matched locally; every step passes plan/exec approval and lands on disk

faq.title: What you're probably wondering.
faq.intro: Honest answers to the questions we get asked most.

items.question: What is Inalpha?
items.answer: Inalpha is an open-source quant agent framework that applies engineering discipline to LLM-driven trading. It treats AI agents not as black-box signal generators, but as code-writing collaborators bounded by hooks, permissions, plan-exec approval, and signed audit trails. The LLM writes the strategy code; the engineering harness signs every decision and enforces every guardrail.

items.question: How is Inalpha different from other quant frameworks like NautilusTrader or vnpy?
items.answer: Inalpha inherits the event-driven kernel design from NautilusTrader (same code for backtest, paper, and live) and the multi-market gateway philosophy from vnpy. What Inalpha adds is an audit-grade middleware layer — hooks, scoped permissions, plan-exec approval tokens, and signed audit trails — inspired by Claude Code's engineering patterns. Traditional quant frameworks focus on execution speed; Inalpha focuses on making every decision provable and replayable.

items.question: Can I trade real money with Inalpha right now?
items.answer: No — and that's deliberate. As of Phase D-11, Inalpha ships an autonomous paper runner: promoted strategies trade a simulated account on live market data, gated by two human checkpoints (you promote, you start), machine-approved through plan/exec, with a full decision-replay log. But every order is matched locally — there is no live brokerage integration, and real-capital trading is out of the current plan. The kernel is designed to extend to live execution later; that is not today's scope. Do not route real money through Inalpha.

items.question: What markets and asset classes does Inalpha cover?
items.answer: Inalpha routes across crypto (CEX via CCXT), US equities, A-shares, Hong Kong, Japan, Korea, Australia, India, UK, Germany, global indices, and FRED macro data — all through one orchestrator and one codebase. Adding a venue gives every agent access to it automatically.

items.question: Is Inalpha free? What's the license?
items.answer: Inalpha is licensed under AGPL-3.0, which means it's free for personal research, academic use, and commercial in-house use. If you modify Inalpha and offer it as a network service, you must release your changes under the same license. For proprietary or closed-source commercial use, please open an issue to discuss a dual license.

items.question: Is there really a fox-spirit fortune draw?
items.answer: Yes — Inari Omikuji, a playful easter egg. Undecided on direction? Cast a hexagram or draw a tarot card for a vantage outside the data. It is entertainment only and hard-walled from decisions: it cannot touch risk, orders, factors, or backtests, never predicts prices, and never emits a buy or sell signal.

cta.title: Star it. Read it. Tear it apart.
cta.sub: Inalpha is alpha-stage and AGPL-3.0. No real money yet — every line is on GitHub.

footer.tagline: Quant agents that evolve under audit.
footer.oracle: an oracle that keeps a ledger · the quant familiar
footer.nameStory: Inari, the fox deity, meets the quant's alpha — a companion that reads your direction and keeps every step on the record.
